data.title: Judge blocks Sandy Hook families' settlement in Alex Jones' bankruptcy
data.description:
Reuters

Judge blocks Sandy Hook families' settlement in Alex Jones' bankruptcy

Dietrich Knauth
February 5, 2025 at 11:21 AM·2 min read
1 / 2

Judge blocks Sandy Hook families' settlement in Alex Jones' bankruptcy

FILE PHOTO: Alex Jones faces second defamation trial over Sandy Hook claims in Connecticut

By Dietrich Knauth

NEW YORK (Reuters) - A U.S. bankruptcy judge on Wednesday blocked a settlement between families who have sued Alex Jones over his false claims about the 2012 Sandy Hook Elementary School mass shooting, saying their attempt to divide the bankrupt conspiracy theorist's assets exceeded his court’s authority.

The decision complicates a proposed sale of Jones' Infowars platform, and could spur divisions between families who sued Jones and won nearly $1.3 billion in Connecticut court cases and those who won $50 million in Texas courts. The two groups of families had proposed a settlement that would guarantee the Texas families a 25% share of Jones' future payments made to the Sandy Hook families, with the Connecticut families taking 75%.

U.S. Bankruptcy Judge Christopher Lopez rejected the settlement at a hearing in Houston, saying the families were asking him to also divide up the assets of Infowars' parent company Free Speech Systems, despite the fact that the company had been dismissed from bankruptcy last year.

"I can't do that," Lopez said. “That case is closed."

Courts in Connecticut and Texas, where some of the families filed their lawsuits, have ruled Jones defamed the families by making repeated false claims that the school shooting, which killed 20 students and six staff members in Newton, Connecticut, was staged as part of a government plot to take guns away from Americans.

The legal judgments drove Jones and Free Speech Systems into bankruptcy in 2022, and Lopez has appointed a bankruptcy trustee to sell Jones’ assets.

Lopez has allowed Free Speech Systems and Infowars to be sold in Jones' ongoing personal bankruptcy. But he said he preferred a “pure sale” of the company’s equity, which is owned by Jones, rather than allowing Infowars’ assets to be broken up and sold separately.

The bankruptcy trustee, Christopher Murray, had previously sought to sell Infowars to the parody news site the Onion, through a bid that was backed by the Connecticut families. Lopez shot down that sale in January, saying that Murray "left money on the table" in order to win the Sandy Hook families’ support.

“We’re not doing that anymore, I don’t trust the process,” Lopez said. “Go sell the equity.”

The families’ attorneys had said the agreement would smooth the way for a sale of Jones’ assets, including Infowars, after their previous disagreements over how to pursue Jones’ assets. In the past, the Texas-based families focused on taking more cash from Jones, while the Connecticut-based families prioritized the shutdown of Jones' Infowars company.

Chris Mattei, an attorney for the Connecticut families, said they remain determined to "enforce every penny of judgments against Alex Jones."

(Reporting by Dietrich Knauth; Editing by Rod Nickel)
data.summary: A U.S. bankruptcy judge on Wednesday blocked a settlement between families who have sued Alex Jones over his false claims about the 2012 Sandy Hook Elementary School mass shooting, saying their attempt to divide the bankrupt conspiracy theorist's assets exceeded his court’s authority.  The decision complicates a proposed sale of Jones' Infowars platform, and could spur divisions between families who sued Jones and won nearly $1.3 billion in Connecticut court cases and those who won $50 million in Texas courts.  The two groups of families had proposed a settlement that would guarantee the Texas families a 25% share of Jones' future payments made to the Sandy Hook families, with the Connecticut families taking 75%.
data.author: Reuters

data.title: Locker looters steal keys, wallets during LA Fitness crime spree in Montco: police
data.description:
WTXF

Locker looters steal keys, wallets during LA Fitness crime spree in Montco: police

Amanda Hurley
February 5, 2025 at 9:59 AM·1 min read

The Brief

  • Keys, wallets and credit cards were stolen from gym lockers in a recent crime spree.

  • At least three suspects were spotted on surveillance footage.

  • Over $1,000 in gift cards were purchased with a stolen credit card.

UPPER MORELAND, Pa. - Gymgoers have recently been the target of a rash of thefts, and police are asking for help to identify the group of suspects.

What we know

Keys, wallets and credit cards have been stolen from locker rooms at LA Fitness in Huntingdon Valley over the past several weeks.

"Thieves lift more than weights," Upper Moreland Township police said.

Two women and one man were spotted on surveillance footage. One of the women was seen with a young girl while inside the gym.

The stolen credit cards were used to buy gift cards on at least two different occasions.

By the numbers

Police say one woman stole keys and a wallet from the locker room, then snatched another wallet from the victim's vehicle.

Another women purchased over $1,000 in gift cards after taking keys from the locker room to steal multiple credit cards from a vehicle, according to police.

What you can do

If you can identify any of these suspects, Upper Moreland Township police are asking you to contact the department.

The Source

Information from this article was provided by the Upper Moreland Township Police Department.
data.summary: Gymgoers have recently been targets in a rash of thefts, and police are asking for help to identify the group of suspects.
data.author: WTXF

data.title: Amber Alert issued for pregnant 16-year-old believed to be with the 40-year-old father
data.description:
ABC News

Amber Alert issued for pregnant 16-year-old believed to be with the 40-year-old father

JULIA REINSTEIN
February 5, 2025 at 11:19 AM·1 min read

An Amber Alert has been issued for a pregnant Wisconsin teen, who police believe is with the 40-year-old man who impregnated her.

Sophia Martha Franklin, 16, is three months pregnant, according to the alert, which was issued Monday.

Gary Day, 40, "is known to be the father of the unborn child," according to the alert.

Franklin has a no-contact order against Day, according to the alert.

Day faces charges of child enticement and abduction, according to a criminal complaint obtained by ABC News.

MORE: Dive team finds remains of North Carolina woman missing for over a year

Franklin previously told police she began speaking with Day online in April, according to the complaint, and later traveled with him to his home in Arkansas.

She was last seen at her home in Beaver Dam, located about an hour northwest of Milwaukee, on Sunday night, the complaint states.

Early Monday morning, Day was seen walking near the family's home on surveillance footage, it states.

Day, who is believed to be driving a black Buick LaCrosse, is known to have used various license plates, according to the Beaver Dam Police Department. The vehicle has been seen with both Arkansas license plate number BBR20L and a Pennsylvania license plate of KGW5186.

Police are asking anyone with information on Sophia's whereabouts to contact them at 920-887-4612 or the Amber Alert tip line at 888-304-3936.

Amber Alert issued for pregnant 16-year-old believed to be with the 40-year-old father originally appeared on abcnews.go.com
data.summary: An Amber Alert has been issued for a pregnant Wisconsin teen, who police believe is with the 40-year-old man who impregnated her.  Gary Day, 40, "is known to be the father of the unborn child," according to the alert.  Franklin has a no-contact order against Day, according to the alert.
data.author: ABC News

data.title: Oklahoma State President Kayse Shrum resigns after almost four years at university
data.description:
The Oklahoman

Oklahoma State President Kayse Shrum resigns after almost four years at university

Murray Evans, The Oklahoman
Updated February 5, 2025 at 1:39 PM·7 min read

Mystery is shrouding the resignation of Kayse Shrum as Oklahoma State University’s president, even as legislators and public leaders praise the work she did as the first female president of one of the state’s two research universities.

The Oklahoman reported Shrum’s expected resignation on Tuesday night, and the OSU/A&M Board of Regents officially announced it Wednesday morning, saying her resignation actually took effect on Monday. The six-paragraph release provided no insight into why Shrum stepped down after less than four years on the job.

In a statement attributed only to the board – not to any one person – the board said, “We extend our deepest gratitude to Dr. Shrum for her dedicated leadership, unwavering commitment to students, and significant contributions to the OSU System. Dr. Shrum has led with passion and vision, championing key initiatives in academics, research and innovation while advancing OSU’s land-grant mission.”

The board said that under Shrum’s leadership, OSU experienced record enrollment, student retention rates, research funding and philanthropic support. It credited her with navigating the latest round of athletic conference realignment after OSU’s rival, the University of Oklahoma, left the Big 12 Conference for the Southeastern Conference.

Shrum also “strengthened its impact in health care and STEM education while expanding access to higher education – ensuring that students of all backgrounds can find opportunities and a home at OSU,” the statement read.

Online news outlet NonDoc reported that Shrum's resignation came as OSU/A&M regents were reviewing transfers of funds appropriated by the Legislature involving OSU's Innovation Foundation. Two executives with the Innovation Foundation, Elizabeth Pollard and Jerome Loughridge, also have resigned, The Oklahoman confirmed.

Jeannette Mendez, OSU’s current provost and senior vice president, will serve as the acting president, until an interim president is named. She’s worked at OSU since 2005.

The regents will hold a special meeting on Friday on OSU's Stillwater campus.

Shrum, 52, became the first woman to lead one of Oklahoma’s two research universities when OSU/A&M regents chose her to succeed Burns Hargis, effective July 1, 2021. Shrum, a doctor of osteopathic medicine, previously had been president of OSU’s Center for Health Sciences.

During Shrum’s tenure, OSU launched initiatives including the Hamm Institute for American Energy, the Oklahoma Aerospace Institute for Research and Education, the Human Performance and Nutrition Research Institute and the OSU Student Farm – an effort to extend the land-grant university's mission to the community.

In December 2023, Shrum launched an expanded OSU Polytech effort, an attempt to address Oklahoma’s high-tech workforce needs, with more than 3,700 students enrolled in 60 programs.

In addition to OSU setting multiple enrollment records during her time as president, the university has maintained flat tuition and mandatory fees the past three years.

Shrum, who’s from Coweta, served in Gov. Kevin Stitt’s cabinet beginning in 2019, when he appointed her secretary of science and innovation. She resigned from the volunteer role in June 2020 after having been part of Stitt’s core team in responding to the COVID-19 pandemic, saying she wanted to focus on developing a safe plan for students to return to campus in the fall.

Stitt issued a statement through a spokesperson that said, "I want to thank Dr. Shrum for her service and I wish her the best in her future endeavors."

State legislators praise Shrum as a 'transformative' leader at OSU

State legislators were nearly universal in their praise for Shrum, including Oklahoma Senate President Pro Tempore Lonnie Paxton, R-Tuttle, who said her impact “extends beyond higher education.”

“Dr. Shrum has been a transformative leader, bringing vision and passion to Oklahoma State University,” Paxton said. “Her dedication to students, faculty and staff has left a lasting impact, and we are deeply grateful for her service. Under her leadership, OSU has strengthened its position as a top-tier research university while remaining dedicated to its land-grant mission of service to Oklahoma.”

House Speaker Kyle Hilbert, R-Bristow, said Shrum’s resignation caught him off guard.

“I greatly appreciate her service to the university and our state,” Hilbert said. “She was instrumental as a member of the governor's cabinet throughout the COVID-19 pandemic in ensuring tests were available for Oklahomans.

“Her tenure as president improved rural health care workforce shortages in establishing a nursing program at OSU-Stillwater and a medical school in Tahlequah. She also faced other challenges such as shepherding my alma mater through conference realignment with the Big 12 going from the brink of disaster to emerging as an undisputed top-four conference in the country. I wish her and (her husband) Darren the best in their future endeavors."

Rep. Trey Caldwell, R-Faxon – the chair of the House Appropriations and Budget Committee – called Shrum’s resignation “a loss for OSU and our state. In recent years we have invested in building up our state's research universities and Dr. Shrum and her team utilized those increased dollars to improve our state's workforce pipeline, especially in medical and STEM fields."

House Postsecondary Education Chair Toni Hasenbeck, R-Elgin, said Shrum “served with distinction as the first female president of a public research institution in Oklahoma and she has much to be proud of from her tenure. … Dr. Shrum's loss will be felt not only at OSU but by the state as a whole."

Rep. Trish Ranson, D-Stillwater – in whose district OSU’s primary campus is located – said Shrum showed “incredible leadership” and “made history” with her tenure. “She made great philanthropic strides and increased enrollment under her tenure. I have greatly enjoyed working with her and I join all of OSU in sincerely thanking her for her service.”

Longtime Oklahoma business leader said Shrum's impact will be long felt at OSU

Longtime oilman Harold Hamm – who worked with Shrum as OSU launched the Hamm Institute for American Energy – also was effusive in his praise.

“Dr. Kayse Shrum’s leadership has left an indelible mark on Oklahoma State University, and her impact will be felt for generations to come,” Hamm said. “Her vision, passion, and dedication to students have transformed the university in countless ways, and her work in on behalf of OSU is a testament to her commitment to innovation, education, and the future of energy in America. Kayse has been a steadfast champion for OSU, always putting students first and ensuring they have the tools, knowledge, and inspiration to lead in their fields. Her brilliance and leadership have not only elevated the university but have also strengthened Oklahoma’s role as a leader in energy and education.”

Outside of the statement from the OSU/A&M board, university officials continued to remain publicly silent on Wednesday. Shrum also has not spoken publicly about her resignation.

"Cowboy pride runs deep in me,” Shrum said on her OSU bio page. “As a Native Oklahoman, I take great pride in the fact that we are one of the most beloved land grant universities in the nation."

When Shrum was hired as president of Oklahoma State University in April 2021, her salary was set at $650,000 annually according to the board of regents. Her benefits included an auto allowance of $16,500 a year, travel reimbursements and memberships to the Stillwater Country Club and Karsten Creek Golf Club.

On social media, Cowboys were quick to voice support for her and the positive changes made on the OSU campus during her tenure.

"Tremendous leadership in less than four years," one X user commented. "Thank you, Dr. Kayse Shrum."

Another X user described Shrum as "a gem of our culture."

Tremendous leadership in less than 4 years.

Thank you, Dr. Kayse Shrum. #GoPokes

— Yves Batoba (@YBatoba) February 5, 2025

Hate to hear it; she has been a gem of our culture.

— Susan (@OSUMeemz) February 5, 2025

Some on social media suggested she may have been forced out through "political strong-arming" and voiced concern about how the Board of Regents may have functioned with Shrum.

I know I said last week that I'm sticking to wrestling...

But this news is incredibly disappointing. Dr. Shrum has been incredible for Oklahoma State. The school's research output and reputation has improved dramatically in her tenure.

If this is political strong arming from… https://t.co/0udXJFvp6d

— Dynasty Defined Podcast (@dynasty_defined) February 5, 2025

Others commented on her leadership ability and previous experience with state government, with many calling for her to run for the state's highest office.

"If Kayse Shrum runs for governor, she has my vote," an X user commented.

If Kayse Shrum runs for governor she has my vote

— Kade (@kadedrummond) February 4, 2025

Kayse Shrum has entered the transfer portal.

— Zachary (@osuzack) February 5, 2025

This story has been updated to add new information and additional photos.

Staff writer Cheyenne Derksen contributed to this report

This article originally appeared on Oklahoman: Oklahoma State President Kayse Shrum resigns: What to know
data.summary: Oklahoma State University President Kayse Shrum has resigned after less than four years in office. The board has begun the search for her replacement.
data.author: The Oklahoman

data.title: Sugar Land road rage incident leaves couple terrified
data.description:
KRIV

Sugar Land road rage incident leaves couple terrified

Leslie DelasBour
February 4, 2025 at 4:28 PM·1 min read

The Brief

  • A road rage driver engaged in a terrifying pursuit of a Houston couple on Beltway 8, attempting to force them off the road and engaging in other reckless maneuvers.

  • The pursuit culminated in a confrontation at an underpass where the driver exited their vehicle and approached the couple's car, making threats and striking the passenger side window.

  • No injuries were reported.

SUGAR LAND, Texas - A terrifying ordeal unfolded Friday night for a Houston couple as they were returning home from the auto show.

Couple terrified after road rage incident

What we know

Eric Corbett recounted a harrowing encounter with a road rage driver who engaged in a dangerous and aggressive pursuit along Beltway 8.

The incident began as the couple was driving home on Beltway 8 when they were targeted by the driver of a blue Dodge Ram. The driver engaged in reckless maneuvers, attempting to force the couple's vehicle off the road, including driving over multiple curbs.

The pursuit continued, with the Dodge Ram driver making several attempts to block the couple's path. The terrifying encounter culminated at the underpass at Beltway 8 and Highway 90 on Main Street, where the Dodge Ram driver exited their vehicle and approached the couple's car.

The driver of the Dodge Ram has been identified as Sean Walker, a former employee of Policy Watch. Walker allegedly stood beside the couple's vehicle, making threats and striking the passenger side window.

Get news, weather and so much more on the new FOX LOCAL app

What we don't know

It's unclear at this time how much damage was caused. It's also unclear if authorities are investigating the incident.

This is a developing story. We'll keep you up-to-date with the latest.
data.summary: A terrifying ordeal unfolded Friday night for a Houston couple as they were returning home from the auto show.
data.author: KRIV

data.title: Nintendo President Weighs In On Switch 2 Pricing As Some Experts Predict $400
data.description:
Kotaku

Nintendo President Weighs In On Switch 2 Pricing As Some Experts Predict $400

Ethan Gach
February 5, 2025 at 9:10 AM·4 min read

The Switch 2 currently faces three big questions following its January reveal: how powerful is it, what games will it have, and what will it cost? Nintendo was asked about that last one in its latest earnings call, and while the company’s president remained cagey, he also said Nintendo will continue to take its reputation for affordability into account when determining the final price, which some experts predict will be $400.

The original Switch launched at $300 back in 2017. Since then, however, inflation has driven up the cost of various components, and console makers this generation have declined their usual hardware price cuts, even raising prices in some regions instead. And while it remains to be seen just how good the specs of the Switch 2 might be, this time around the device will also be partially competing with higher-end PC gaming handhelds like the Steam Deck.

“We are aware that inflation is currently rising and that the exchange rate environment has changed significantly since the launch of the Nintendo Switch in 2017,” Nintendo president Shuntaro Furukawa told investors in the company’s third-quarter earnings call this week, according to a translation by Nintendo Everything. “We also need to consider the affordable prices that customers expect from Nintendo products. When considering the price of our products, we believe that it is necessary to consider these factors from multiple angles. At this time, we cannot announce the specific price of the Nintendo Switch 2, but we are considering it taking into account various points.”

There will certainly be pressure to price the Switch 2 higher than its predecessor, especially because Nintendo traditionally tries to make profit off of its hardware rather than selling it under market value as a loss-leader for selling software. At the same time, Furukawa’s remarks suggest that, despite the wild popularity of the original Switch, Nintendo knows it risks pricing itself into irrelevance if it aims too high.

What might the Switch 2 cost?

So what will the Switch 2 actually cost? Last month, IGN interviewed several industry analysts for their expert opinions. While some think Nintendo could charge as much as $500 for the new device, many are banking on it costing $400. This was the “sweet spot” for Rhys Elliot of MIDiA, and Ampere Analysis’ Piers Harding-Rolls told IGN he was currently basing his sales projections on the same number. At the same time, diehard fans upgrading in the first year would probably be willing to pay more. “Early adopters won’t be price sensitive, and prices can always be lowered,” Circana’s Mat Piscatella said. “Of course, people will want it to be lower than whatever it is it will be announced at.”

And what does history tell us Nintendo will do? Polygon assembled a chart of inflation-adjusted prices of all of the company’s consoles. Starting with the launch of the Nintendo 64, the price has mostly hung around $350 to $400. That seems like the minimum price this time around considering the Switch OLED itself is still $350, and while the Switch 2 is rumored to have a cheaper LCD screen, its other features, including the Joy-Con with mouse-like functionality, are a lot beefier this time around.

Read More: 21 Games We’d Love To See On The Nintendo Switch 2

Polygon also notes that Nintendo has a history of trying to undercut its competition when it comes to price. This is the tradition of “affordability” Furukawa was referencing in this week’s call. But that will be harder to do late into a console generation where the disc-less PS5 is $450 (up from its 2020 launch price of $400). The current cheapest Steam Deck is also $400, though you can also get refurbished ones for under $300 when they’re available.

My own gut tells me that making the Switch 2 cost $400 is the logical play here, with $450 the absolute max the company can charge, unless it debuts something truly stunning during its April Switch 2 Direct that will get people excited to upgrade. I showed my kids the original Switch 2 reveal and they haven’t asked about it again at all, content to keep toiling away in The Legend of Zelda: Tears of the Kingdom and Super Mario Bros. Wonder.

Families without a parent that writes about video games for a living might feel similarly unmoved about upgrading early if the price is too high. But all of that will depend on everything Nintendo still hasn’t told us about it’s next console. We’ll have to wait a couple more months for that info too.

.

For the latest news, Facebook, Twitter and Instagram.
data.summary: The Switch 2 currently faces three big questions following its January reveal: how powerful is it, what games will it have, and what will it cost? Nintendo was asked about that last one in its latest earnings call, and while the company’s president remained cagey, he also said Nintendo will continue to take its reputation for affordability into account when determining the final price, which some experts predict will be $400.
data.author: Kotaku

data.title: Millions may be eligible for $6,000 in data breach settlement
data.description:
Digital Trends

Millions may be eligible for $6,000 in data breach settlement

Judy Sanhz
February 4, 2025 at 4:34 PM·2 min read

Millions of Americans may be eligible for up to $6,000 from a major data breach settlement —but they must act fast, as the deadline is this Monday, Newsweek reports.

Arthur J. Gallagher & Co., a global insurance brokerage and risk management firm, agreed to a $21 million settlement after hackers stole sensitive data in a data breach between June 3 and September 26, 2020. Even more alarming, the firm notified affected Americans almost a year after the data breach. Those affected had no idea their Social Security numbers, personal identifying information, personal health information, usernames, passwords, medical information, biometric information, and credit card information were taken.

You are eligible for a claim if you were with the firm between June and September of 2020. However, you must prove you suffered monetary damages from the data breach to be eligible for the compensation. You can file the claim on the settlement website, ajgdatasettlement.com, by Monday, February 10. Alternatively, you can mail out your claim, and as long as it has a postmark by February 10 or earlier, it should be accepted. Additionally, ensure you supply proof of identity and any documents that prove you suffered financial losses or expenses because of the breach. For example, you can provide documents such as bank statements, receipts for identity theft protection services, or bills.

Christopher E. Roberts, class action attorney at Butsch Roberts & Associates LLC, told Newsweek: “This settlement is significant for two primary reasons. First, this settlement further highlights how prevalent data breaches are and continue to be. Data breach class actions are one of the largest, if not the largest, types of class action cases that are currently being filed.

Second, this settlement further highlights the importance of companies developing appropriate safeguards to safeguard consumers’ data… even large companies, even one of the world’s largest insurance brokerages, can be susceptible to data breaches.”

The compensation is a maximum of $6,000, including credit monitoring and identity theft insurance, but the amount can vary depending on the number of people who submit valid claims. It may not be the worst data breach in history, but it shows how these types of attacks continue to happen and put our data at risk.
data.summary: Those affected by the Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. data breach have little time left to claim their piece of the pie. Discover what you can do to not miss out.
data.author: Digital Trends

data.title: RI is expected to get snow tomorrow. Here's how much, where parking bans are issued for
data.description:
The Providence Journal

RI is expected to get snow tomorrow. Here's how much, where parking bans are issued for

Katie Landeck, USA TODAY NETWORK - New England
Updated February 5, 2025 at 9:37 AM·1 min read

It's time to start figuring out where to park your car ahead of the storm on Thursday as parking bans are being issued.

Tomorrow, Rhode Island is expected to get 2 to 3 inches of snow across most of the state, and between 1 to 2 inches along the coastline, according to the National Weather Service. A light glaze from ice is also possible.

A winter weather advisory has been issued for the state from 7 a.m. to 7 p.m. The snow is expected to start mid-morning.

"Plan on slippery road conditions. The hazardous conditions could impact the Thursday morning and evening commutes," the National Weather Service said.

Ahead of the storm, municipalities have started to issue parking bans. Here's where:

RI Parking Bans for Feb. 6

This list will be updates as more parking bans are announced.

  • Woonsocket: Parking ban in affect from 7 a.m. to 11 p.m.

  • North Kingstown: Parking ban in affect from 8 a.m. to 8 p.m.

  • North Smithfield: Parking ban for Thursday morning.

  • Portsmouth: Parking ban from 7 a.m. to 7 p.m.

  • Tiverton:  Parking ban from 5 a.m. to 5 p.m. on North/East sides.

More: Does your town have a parking ban? Here's where to leave your car during a snowstorm in RI

RI Snow forecast prediction map

Southern New England is expected to see between 1 and 4 inches of snow in the storm on Thursday.

Most of Rhode Island is expected to see 2 to 3 inches. Northern Massachusetts is expected to see the heavier snowfall.

This article originally appeared on The Providence Journal: RI Snow: See list of where parking bans have been issued for Thursday
data.summary: A winter weather advisory has been issued from 7 a.m. to 7 p.m. on Thursday.
data.author: The Providence Journal

data.title: USPS accepting Chinese packages again: Alibaba, PDD, JD.com react
data.description:
Yahoo Finance Video

USPS accepting Chinese packages again: Alibaba, PDD, JD.com react

Yahoo Finance
February 5, 2025 at 8:10 AM

Chinese e-commerce platforms Pinduoduo (PDD), JD.com (JD), and Alibaba (BABA) are paring back stock losses, while remaining in the red, after the United States Postal Service (USPS) reversed its decision and will continue to accept inbound packages from China.

Yahoo Finance reporter Jordan Weissmann covers the latest developments.
